1	---
2	name: lean-auditor
3	description: Whole-project read-only audit of all .lean code, with no strategy bias. Per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, bad Lean practices. Critical pushback on excuse-comments ("temporary wrong def", "will fix later") — treats them as red flags, not workflow.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [review]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every review phase. The audit warning
11	    fires if I am skipped without a recorded rationale.
12	
13	    **You may skip me this iter when ALL of:**
14	      - no `.lean` file under the project tree was modified this iter
15	        (the prover phase committed no edits — check via
16	        `git diff --stat HEAD~1 -- '*.lean'`);
17	      - my prior verdict had no must-fix-this-iter findings.
18	
19	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`.
20	  - Pass me as LITTLE strategic context as possible. Do NOT include
21	    STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, or "this is what we are trying to prove"
22	    framing in the directive. My value depends on auditing the Lean
23	    *without* bias toward what the strategy claims should be true.
24	  - Acceptable directive content: a list of files, optional focus areas
25	    (e.g. "pay extra attention to Algebra/"), and the absolute paths
26	    to read. That's it.
27	  - My output is a per-file checklist plus a flagged-issues block. Use
28	    it to seed `recommendations.md` and (when severe) trigger a
29	    structural-refactor directive (via the catalog's refactor
30	    subagent) next iter.
31	  - I audit the Lean as Lean. The catalog may include a separate
32	    Lean ↔ blueprint checker that compares Lean against the
33	    chapter — run both for full coverage when both are present.
34	---
35	
36	# Lean Auditor
37	
38	You read **every `.lean` file in the project** and produce a per-file checklist of outdated comments, suspect definitions, dead-end proofs, and bad Lean practices. You are **read-only** and your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
39	
40	You are **highly recommended in the review phase**: the review agent dispatches you whenever any `.lean` file has been modified this iter, OR whenever the prior verdict had live must-fix findings. Your output flags issues the prover and plan agents may have missed.
41	
42	## Stance: no strategic bias
43	
44	You audit the Lean **as Lean**. Your directive will NOT include the project strategy, the blueprint, or "what we are trying to prove" — and that is intentional. The point is to detect mathematically-wrong definitions and dead-end proofs *without* being swayed by what the project assumes should be true. A definition is wrong if it is wrong by reading the Lean alone (e.g. its body contradicts standard mathematical usage of the named concept); you do not need to know the strategy to spot that.
45	
46	This stance is also what makes you **critical of excuse-comments**. Lean-side comments like:
47	
48	- `-- We decided to use a temporary wrong definition; will fix it later.`
49	- `-- This proof is incomplete but works for now.`
50	- `-- TODO: replace before merging.`
51	- `-- Stand-in definition until we figure out the right one.`
52	
53	are RED FLAGS, not project workflow. Authors writing such comments are admitting the code is wrong; the comments cannot be allowed to silence the alarm. Flag every such comment with severity **major** at minimum — and **critical** when the named declaration is load-bearing.
54	
55	## Directive Format
56	
57	```markdown
58	# Lean Auditor Directive
59	
60	## Slug
61	<slug>
62	
63	## Scope (files)
64	<"all" by default. Optionally a list of file paths if the review agent wants to narrow scope; never expand beyond the project.>
65	
66	## Focus areas (optional)
67	<directories or namespaces to pay extra attention to. Bias toward thoroughness — do not skip the rest of the project just because focus is named.>
68	
69	## Known issues
70	<things the review agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
71	```
72	
73	The directive **does not** include STRATEGY.md, PROGRESS.md, references, or a description of what the project is trying to prove. That is by design.
74	
75	## What you do
76	
77	1. Read your directive.
78	2. **List every `.lean` file** under the project. Use `find` or `ls -R` — do not rely on import graphs (some files may be temporarily orphaned).
79	3. **For each file** (every one, no scope shortcuts):
80	   - Read the whole file.
81	   - For each declaration: is the signature reasonable as Lean? Is the body suspect (uses `axiom`, `Classical.choice _`, `sorry` with a body that looks like fake content, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial claim)?
82	   - For each comment: is it stale (referencing removed code or old strategies)? Is it an *excuse-comment* (see above)? Is it accurate about the code below it?
83	   - For each proof: is the tactic chain reasonable for the goal type, or does it look like it is going in the wrong direction (e.g. unfolding to manipulate syntax instead of using the available structure lemmas)?
84	   - Note any bad Lean practices: misuse of universe polymorphism, redundant typeclasses, deprecated tactics, anti-patterns like proving `False`-style lemmas to bypass infrastructure.
85	4. **Produce a per-file checklist** + a flagged-issues block grouped by severity.
86	
87	You may use:
88	- `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools (read-only): hover, signature lookup, diagnostics. Useful to verify a declaration's actual type.
89	- `sorry_analyzer` if available, to get sorry counts per file.
90	- Standard `Read` / `Grep` for code reading.
91	
92	You may NOT modify any project file. Even when a fix is obvious.
93	
94	## Report format
95	
96	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/lean-auditor-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path under `task_results/<parent-slug>/` when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
97	
98	```markdown
99	# Lean Audit Report
100	
101	## Slug
102	<slug>
103	
104	## Iteration
105	<NNN>
106	
107	## Scope
108	- files audited: <N>
109	- files skipped (per directive): <N> — <reason>
110	
111	## Per-file checklist
112	
113	### <path/to/file.lean>
114	- **outdated comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
115	- **suspect definitions**: <N flagged> (or "none")
116	- **dead-end proofs**: <N flagged> (or "none")
117	- **bad practices**: <N flagged> (or "none")
118	- **excuse-comments**: <N flagged> (or "none")
119	- **notes**:
120	  - <one bullet per finding, naming line number where relevant>
121	  - ...
122	
123	### <next file>
124	- ...
125	
126	(One block per file. Cover every file, including the ones with no findings — they get `none` everywhere and `notes: -`.)
127	
128	## Must-fix-this-iter
129	
130	Apply verbatim. Every one of the following lands here automatically, no exceptions, no under-classification:
131	
132	- **Excuse-comments** on any declaration: `-- TODO replace with real def`, `-- placeholder`, `-- temporary`, `-- wrong but works`, `-- will fix later`. These are admissions that the code is wrong; treat them as wrong.
133	- **Weakened-wrong definitions**: a `def Foo := <something structurally different>` standing in for the real concept (e.g. `Scheme.LineBundle := CommRing.Pic …` when the real definition is an invertible quasi-coherent sheaf).
134	- **Parallel APIs of existing Mathlib**: a project file that copy-and-modifies a Mathlib definition (changing one type-class or argument) instead of instancing the existing Mathlib version. Bridge lemmas needed to consume it are the symptom.
135	- **Suspect bodies on substantive claims**: `:= sorry` on a load-bearing claim, `:= True`, `:= rfl` on a non-trivial statement, `:= Classical.choice _` without authorization.
136	- **Axioms** on non-trivial claims that the project hasn't explicitly authorized in the strategy.
137	
138	A finding that meets any of the above lands at must-fix-this-iter — even if classifying it as "major" would feel more diplomatic. Soft severity is how wrong code hardens into the project.
139	
140	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>. Why must-fix: <one line>.
141	- ...
142	
143	## Major
144	
145	Findings that are real issues but don't meet the must-fix bar — typically stale comments that don't actively mislead, naming drift, minor code smells.
146	
147	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
148	- ...
149	
150	## Minor
151	
152	Low-impact observations.
153	
154	- `<file>:<line>` — <description>.
155	- ...
156	
157	## Excuse-comments (always called out separately)
158	
159	If any excuse-comments were flagged, list each one verbatim with its file:line. These deserve special visibility because they document the project lying to itself.
160	
161	- `Foo/Bar.lean:42`: "TODO: replace with the real definition" (attached to `Foo.bar`, which is a load-bearing definition in `Baz.lean`). Severity: critical.
162	- ...
163	
164	## Severity summary
165	
166	- **must-fix-this-iter**: <N> — these block downstream work in their files until addressed (see plan.md's per-file gate).
167	- **major**: <N>
168	- **minor**: <N>
169	- **excuse-comments**: <N> (also counted under must-fix-this-iter above; called out separately because they document the project lying to itself).
170	
171	Overall verdict: one sentence.
172	```
173	
174	**Strict severity reminder.** The plan agent's gate treats must-fix-this-iter as blocking. If you under-classify a wrong-decision finding to keep the loop's momentum, you are working against the project — wrong code accumulates faster than reviewers can catch it later. When in doubt, classify higher, not lower.
175	
176	## Return value
177	
178	Your final assistant message:
179	
180	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> files audited, <M> issues (critical/major/minor: a/b/c)`
181	- The path to your full report.
182	
183	## Reminders
184	
185	- **Read every file. No scope shortcuts.**
186	- **No strategic context.** Your audit is unbiased by design.
187	- **You are read-only.** No project source, no blueprint, no state files (except your own report).
188	- **Excuse-comments are red flags.** Critical or major severity, every time.
189	- **Per-file checklist is the primary output.** Cover every file; the format is fixed.
190

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

6	import Mathlib
7	import AlgebraicJacobian.Genus
8	import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.WeilDivisor
9	import AlgebraicJacobian.RiemannRoch.OCofP
10	
11	/-!
12	# The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor (RR.2_*)
13	
14	This file is the **RR.2_*** satellite sub-build chapter for the project's
15	headline `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (the "smooth proper geometrically
16	irreducible genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is isomorphic to `ℙ¹`" lemma in
17	`AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity`).
18	
19	Together with `RR.1` (`WeilDivisor.lean`), `RR.2`
20	(`RRFormula.lean`), `RR.3` (`OCofP.lean`), and `RR.4`
21	(`RationalCurveIso.lean`), the present chapter forms the four-stage
22	sub-build closing the project's headline **RR bridge**.
23	
24	The Hartshorne IV.1.3.5 chain consumed by `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` of
25	`AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean` relies on the invertible-sheaf functor
26	`𝒪_C(-) : Div(C) → 𝐒𝐡(C, 𝐌𝐨𝐝_k̄)` sending a Weil divisor
27	`D = Σᵢ nᵢ · [Pᵢ]` to its associated invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` on `C`.
28	
29	This file hosts the four pinned declarations of the chapter
30	`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`:
31	
32	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` — the invertible sheaf
33	   `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth proper curve `C / k̄`,
34	   realised as Hartshorne's subsheaf of the function-field constant
35	   sheaf `K_C` (II §6 p. 144). Replaces the iter-174 typed-`sorry`
36	   placeholder currently living at `RiemannRoch/RRFormula.lean:168`.
37	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_zero` — the zero
38	   divisor `D = 0` gives the structure sheaf `𝒪_C(0) = 𝒪_C`.
39	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_singlePoint` — the
40	   closed-point specialisation `𝒪_C([P]) = lineBundleAtClosedPoint P`
41	   of `RR.3` (`OCofP.lean`).
42	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf_ses_single_add` — the
43	   Hartshorne IV.1.3 inductive-step short exact sequence
44	   `0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(D + [P]) → k(P) → 0` consumed by Lane H's
45	   `RRFormula` induction.
46

82	/-! ## §1. The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor -/
83	
84	namespace Scheme.WeilDivisor
85	
86	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar] [IsAlgClosed kbar]
87	  {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper C.hom]
88	  [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
89	  [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom] [IsIntegral C.left]
90	
91	/-- **The invertible sheaf `𝒪_C(D)` of a Weil divisor `D` on a smooth
92	proper curve `C / k̄`** (Hartshorne II §6 p. 144, definition of `ℒ(D)`).
93	
94	For a Weil divisor `D = Σ_Q n_Q · [Q] ∈ Div(C)` on a smooth proper
95	geometrically irreducible curve `C / k̄`, the invertible sheaf
96	`𝒪_C(D) := ℒ(D)` is the sub-`𝒪_C`-module of the function-field constant
97	sheaf `𝒦_C ≅ K(C)` (Hartshorne Proposition 6.15: on an integral scheme
98	the sheaf of total quotient rings is the constant sheaf at the function
99	field) defined section-wise on each open `U ⊆ C` as
100	`Γ(U, 𝒪_C(D)) = { f ∈ K(C) | f = 0 ∨ ord_Q(f) ≥ −n_Q ∀ prime divisor Q
101	∈ U }`, with restriction along `V ⊆ U` given by the identity on `K(C)`.
102	
103	The signature returns a `Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
104	(ModuleCat.{u} kbar)`: the same `ModuleCat k̄`-flavoured sheaf carrier used
105	by the project's `Scheme.HModule` cohomology pipeline (so that `H⁰` and
106	`H¹` of `𝒪_C(D)` are accessible via
107	`Scheme.HModule kbar (sheafOf D) 0/1`).
108	
109	**iter-183 Lane K status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. The iter-184+
110	body recipe (per chapter `RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex` §"Sheaf-property
111	correctness"): per-open `Submodule kbar K(C)` cut out by the order
112	conditions (the closure proofs reduce to `Ring.ordFrac`-multiplicativity
113	and the non-archimedean inequality of the DVR valuation at each prime
114	divisor); presheaf functor via the identity-on-`K(C)` restriction; sheaf
115	property via gluing-by-stalks (stalk-locality of the order conditions at
116	each prime divisor). The construction parallels the project's existing
117	`Scheme.toModuleKPresheaf` / `toModuleKPresheaf_isSheaf` template in
118	`AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/StructureSheafModuleK/`.
119	
120	**Coordination with `RRFormula.lean:168`.** The iter-174 typed-`sorry`
121	placeholder `Scheme.WeilDivisor.sheafOf` in `RRFormula.lean` is
122	slated to be retired (Lane H) by re-export of this declaration. Both
123	files compile in isolation; cross-imports are coordinated by Lane H.
124	
125	Blueprint reference: `def:sheafOf` (Hartshorne II §6 p. 144;
126	Propositions 6.13, 6.15; Remark 6.17.1).
127	
128	**iter-185 Lane K body fragment.** The case `D = 0` is implemented on the
129	nose as the structure sheaf `Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C` (Hartshorne's
130	``\(\mathcal O_C(0) = \mathcal O_C\)'' identification; chapter
131	`RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, Lemma `lem:sheafOf_zero`). The general case
132	remains an honest typed `sorry` pending the iter-186+ Hartshorne
133	subsheaf-of-`K_C` construction described in the docstring above. The
134	`if`-branching uses `Classical.dec` (via `open Classical in`) on the
135	`WeilDivisor = (PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ)` carrier, which has no canonical
136	decidable equality. -/
137	noncomputable def sheafOf (D : C.left.WeilDivisor) :
138	    Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
139	      (ModuleCat.{u} kbar) :=
140	  open Classical in
141	  if D = 0 then Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C else sorry
142	
143	/-! ## §2. Immediate corollaries -/
144	
145	/-- **Sheaf of the zero divisor is the structure sheaf**
146	(chapter `RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, Lemma `sheafOf_zero`).
147	
148	At `D = 0` the coefficient `n_Q = 0` at every prime divisor `Q`, so the
149	section condition of `sheafOf` reduces to the standard
150	"non-negative-order = regular" identification of the structure sheaf on
151	an integral scheme (Hartshorne II §6 immediately before Proposition 6.11).
152	Both sides are sub-`𝒪_C`-modules of `𝒦_C` with identity restriction
153	maps, so the equality of presheaves promotes to an equality of sheaves
154	of `ModuleCat k̄`-modules.
155	
156	**iter-183 Lane K status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. The body closes
157	on iter-184+ closure of `sheafOf` together with the standard `Γ(U, 𝒪_C)`
158	identification.
159	
160	**iter-185 Lane K body**: closes via the explicit `D = 0` branch in the
161	`sheafOf` def above (modified iter-185 to land the structure-sheaf value
162	on the `0` divisor on the nose). Unfolding `sheafOf` exposes the
163	`if 0 = 0 then toModuleKSheaf C else sorry` and `if_pos rfl` picks the
164	true branch.
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `lem:sheafOf_zero`. -/
167	lemma sheafOf_zero :
168	    sheafOf (C := C) (0 : C.left.WeilDivisor) = Scheme.toModuleKSheaf C := by
169	  unfold sheafOf
170	  exact if_pos rfl
171	
172	/-- **Sheaf at a single closed point is the line bundle of `RR.3`**
173	(chapter `RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, Lemma `sheafOf_singlePoint`).
174	
175	For a closed point `P ∈ C` viewed as a Weil divisor `[P] ∈ Div(C)` via
176	`Scheme.WeilDivisor.ofClosedPoint`, the invertible sheaf `𝒪_C([P])` of
177	`sheafOf` agrees on the nose with the closed-point line bundle
178	`lineBundleAtClosedPoint P` of `RR.3` (`OCofP.lean`).
179	
180	The agreement is by unfolding both definitions: at `D = [P]`, the order
181	conditions of `sheafOf` (`ord_Q(f) ≥ 0` for `Q ≠ P`, `ord_P(f) ≥ −1`)
182	are exactly those characterising sections of `lineBundleAtClosedPoint P`
183	(via `lineBundleAtClosedPoint_globalSections_iff` of `OCofP.lean`).
184	
185	**iter-183 Lane K status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. Closes on
186	iter-184+ closure of both `sheafOf` and `lineBundleAtClosedPoint`.
187	
188	Blueprint reference: `lem:sheafOf_singlePoint`. -/
189	lemma sheafOf_singlePoint (P : C.left) (hP : IsClosed ({P} : Set C.left))
190	    (hPcoh : Order.coheight P = 1) :
191	    sheafOf (C := C) (ofClosedPoint P hP) =
192	      lineBundleAtClosedPoint (C := C) P hP hPcoh := by
193	  sorry
194	
195	/-- **Short exact sequence for `D ↝ D + [P]`** (Hartshorne IV.1.3
196	inductive step, p. 296; chapter `RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex`, Lemma
197	`sheafOf_ses_single_add`).
198	
199	For a Weil divisor `D ∈ Div(C)` and a prime divisor `P` of `C`
200	(equivalently, a closed point on a smooth curve), the inclusion
201	`𝒪_C(D) ↪ 𝒪_C(D + [P])` of sub-`𝒪_C`-modules of `𝒦_C` fits into a short
202	exact sequence
203	`0 → 𝒪_C(D) → 𝒪_C(D + [P]) → k(P) → 0`
204	in `Sh(C, 𝐌𝐨𝐝_k̄)`, where `k(P)` is the skyscraper sheaf at `P` with
205	stalk `k̄`.
206	
207	The statement bundles the existence of a `ShortComplex` whose `X₁ ↦ X₂`
208	arm matches `sheafOf D ↪ sheafOf (Finsupp.single P 1 + D)` and that is
209	`ShortExact` (mono + epi + exact). The third term `X₃` is the
210	skyscraper `k(P)` — encoded here as a (nonempty) isomorphism class with
211	`Mathlib`'s `skyscraperSheaf` (decidability of `P.point ∈ U` supplied
212	classically). Lane H's `RRFormula` consumes this sequence via
213	χ-additivity to derive `χ(𝒪_C(D + [P])) = χ(𝒪_C(D)) + 1`.
214	
215	**iter-183 Lane K status** — Tier-3 honest typed sorry. The iter-184+
216	body recipe (per chapter `RiemannRoch_OcOfD.tex` §"Immediate
217	corollaries", Beat 1 + Beat 2): build the ideal-sheaf SES
218	`0 → 𝒪_C(−[P]) → 𝒪_C → k(P) → 0`, tensor with `𝒪_C(D + [P])` (which is
219	locally free of rank 1, hence the tensor is exact), and identify the
220	three terms via `𝒪_C(−[P]) ⊗ 𝒪_C(D + [P]) ≅ 𝒪_C(D)` (Hartshorne 6.13(b)),
221	`𝒪_C ⊗ 𝒪_C(D + [P]) ≅ 𝒪_C(D + [P])` (tensor unit), and
222	`k(P) ⊗ 𝒪_C(D + [P]) ≅ k(P)` (rank-1 stalk at `P`).
223	
224	The argument `D` carries the underlying `Finsupp` type rather than
225	`Scheme.WeilDivisor` so that the `Finsupp.single P 1 + D` term elaborates
226	cleanly (the addition is in the unambiguous Finsupp instance); the result
227	is consumed via the definitional equality `WeilDivisor = (PrimeDivisor →₀
228	ℤ)`, matching the pattern of `Scheme.eulerCharacteristic_sheafOf_single_add`
229	in `RRFormula.lean`.
230	
231	Blueprint reference: `lem:sheafOf_ses_single_add` (Hartshorne IV.1, p. 296). -/
232	theorem sheafOf_ses_single_add
233	    (D : C.left.PrimeDivisor →₀ ℤ) (P : C.left.PrimeDivisor) :
234	    ∃ S : CategoryTheory.ShortComplex
235	        (Sheaf (Opens.grothendieckTopology C.left.toTopCat)
236	          (ModuleCat.{u} kbar)),
237	      S.ShortExact ∧
238	      S.X₁ = sheafOf (C := C) D ∧
239	      S.X₂ = sheafOf (C := C) (Finsupp.single P 1 + D) ∧
240	      Nonempty (S.X₃ ≅ skyscraperSheaf (C := ModuleCat.{u} kbar)
241	        P.point (ModuleCat.of kbar kbar)) := by
242	  sorry
243	
244	end Scheme.WeilDivisor
245	
246	end AlgebraicGeometry

10	/-!
11	# Abelian-variety rigidity: genus-`0` final assembly
12	
13	This file is the **genus-`0` final layer** of the project's committed characteristic-free
14	route (route (c)) to abelian-variety rigidity. It assembles the headline
15	`rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` — the char-free replacement (no `[CharZero kbar]`) for
16	`AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar` of `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar` (which remains in
17	tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and still carries `[CharZero]`).
18	
19	The abstract Mumford Rigidity Lemma chain (`rigidity_lemma`) and the two Milne §I.1 corollaries
20	(`hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity` = Cor 1.5, `av_regularMap_isHom_of_zero` = Cor 1.2) it
21	implies live **upstream** in `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityLemma`, which is imported here. That
22	chain is PROVEN axiom-clean (iters 157–162); this file consumes it.
23	
24	The final assembly has three links specific to the genus-`0` route:
25	
26	1. `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` — every morphism `ℙ¹ → A` into an abelian variety is
27	   constant. Proved (route resolved iter-164) by the **𝔾ₘ-scaling shortcut**: the total scaling
28	   action `σ_× : ℙ¹ × 𝔾ₘ → ℙ¹`, `(x, λ) ↦ λx`, feeds the proven Cor 1.5
29	   (`hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity`) — NO theorem of the cube, NO Milne Thm 3.2, NO
30	   `Hom(𝔾ₐ, A) = 0`, char-general. (Still a scaffold `sorry` pending the concrete ℙ¹/𝔾ₘ/σ_× infra.)
31	2. `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` — a smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
32	   isomorphic to `ℙ¹` (blocked on Riemann–Roch).
33	3. `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` — THE HEADLINE consumed by `genusZeroWitness`.
34	
35	The file also exposes the project-local bridge `iotaGm_isDominant` — the dominance of the
36	canonical `Gm ↪ ℙ¹` inclusion — which becomes provable once Lane A
37	(`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus0BaseObjects`) ships the concrete chartwise body of `gmScalingP1`.
38	
39	See `blueprint/src/chapters/AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex` for the informal sketches and sources
40	(Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch. II §6; Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, Prop. 3.10;
41	Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Example IV.1.3.5).
42	
43	## Encoding notes
44	
45	Mathlib `b80f227` packages no `ℙ¹` as a `Scheme`, so the projective line is encoded by its
46	abstract characterisation: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible `Over (Spec (.of kbar))` of
47	relative dimension `1` with `genus = 0` (see
48	`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus0BaseObjects.ProjectiveLineBar`).
49	The signatures of declarations 1–3 are **provisional** (`SCAFFOLD` comments mark them); the prover
50	may refine the encoding when the bodies are filled. Declaration 3 is pinned verbatim to
51	`rigidity_over_kbar`'s signature minus `[CharZero kbar]`, because it is the exact signature the
52	consumer (`genusZeroWitness.key`) needs.
53	-/

63	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
64	
65	/-! ### Iter-167 dominance bridge for the canonical `Gm ↪ ℙ¹` map
66	
67	The four product / Proj instances `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const_aux` needs
68	(`GeometricallyIrreducible`, `LocallyOfFiniteType`, `IsReduced` of the product, and
69	`IsReduced (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left`) all ship from Lane A
70	(`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus0BaseObjects`) as the instances `projGm_geomIrred`,
71	`projGm_locallyOfFiniteType`, `projGm_isReduced`, and `projectiveLineBar_isReduced`. The
72	helper resolves them all by `infer_instance`.

74	The one remaining bridge — dominance of the canonical inclusion `ι : Gm ⟶ ℙ¹` — is
75	file-local because its proof depends on the concrete chartwise body of `gmScalingP1` (still
76	a Lane A scaffold sorry through iter-167). It is exposed here as a named top-level lemma

78	
79	/-- **Sub-task (b) helper (iter-183 Lane E):** the `k̄`-point `onePt = [1:1] ∈ ℙ¹` factors
80	through the chart-1 affine open `D₊(X 1) ⊆ ℙ¹` via `Proj.awayι (X 1)`.
81	
82	**Mathematical content.** `onePt.left = Proj.fromOfGlobalSections 𝒜 (evalIntoGlobal v) _`
83	with `v 0 = v 1 = 1`. Since `evalIntoGlobal v (X 1) = (ΓSpecIso k̄).inv 1 = 1` is a unit in
84	`Γ(Spec k̄, ⊤)`, the preimage of `D₊(X 1) ⊆ Proj 𝒜` under `onePt.left` is
85	`(Spec k̄).basicOpen 1 = ⊤`. Hence by `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_morphismRestrict` the
86	restriction of `onePt.left` to `D₊(X 1)` is the entire morphism (modulo the trivial
87	`(Spec k̄)|_⊤ ≅ Spec k̄` identification), so `onePt.left = r_1 ≫ Proj.awayι (X 1)` for
88	the unique `r_1 : Spec k̄ ⟶ Spec(Away 𝒜 (X 1))` corresponding to the ring map
89	`Away 𝒜 (X 1) →+* k̄`, `[a/X_1^n] ↦ eval_v(a)`.
90	
91	The ring map is concretely
92	`(MvPolynomial.eval (fun _ : Unit => (1:kbar))).comp (homogeneousLocalizationAwayIso 1).toRingHom`,
93	i.e. through the chart-ring iso send `[X_0/X_1] ↦ 1` and constants `[r] ↦ r`.
94	
95	**Status (iter-184 closure — Lane E HARD BAR, axiom-clean Tier-1).** Witness provided via
96	`IsOpenImmersion.lift (Proj.awayι (X 1)) onePt.left h_range`; the factorisation equation
97	discharges via `IsOpenImmersion.lift_fac`. The range containment chain landed iter-184:
98	(i) `Scheme.Hom.coe_opensRange` + `Proj.opensRange_awayι` rewrite the RHS into
99	`↑(Proj.basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1))`; (ii) pointwise destructure to `onePt.left x ∈ basicOpen`;
100	(iii) `Scheme.Hom.mem_preimage` + `change` expose `onePt.left = fromOfGlobalSections 𝒜 φ hφ`
101	(`pointOfVec` unfolds through `Over.homMk` even though `evalIntoGlobal`/`pointOfVec` are
102	private — defeq is unaffected by name visibility); (iv) `fromOfGlobalSections_preimage_basicOpen`
103	reduces to `(Spec k̄).basicOpen ((ΓSpecIso k̄).inv.hom (eval (·=1) (X 1)))`;
104	(v) `Scheme.basicOpen_of_isUnit` + `simp` closes via `eval_X` + `map_one` + `isUnit_one`.
105	Axiom-clean: `#print axioms` = `{propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}`. -/
106	private lemma iotaGm_onePt_chart1_factor (kbar : Type u) [Field kbar] :
107	    ∃ r_1 : Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar) ⟶
108	        Spec (CommRingCat.of (HomogeneousLocalization.Away
109	          (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)
110	          ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
111	            Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) 1))),
112	      r_1 ≫ Proj.awayι (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)
113	          ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
114	            Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) 1)
115	          (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_fDeg kbar 1)
116	          (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_hm 1) =
117	        (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left := by
118	  -- Strategy: use `IsOpenImmersion.lift` against the open immersion `awayι (X 1)`. The
119	  -- range containment `Set.range onePt.left ⊆ Set.range (awayι (X 1)) = D₊(X 1)` is the
120	  -- residual content: `onePt`'s closed-point image `[1:1]` lies in `D₊(X 1)` because
121	  -- `v 1 = 1 ≠ 0`.
122	  refine ⟨IsOpenImmersion.lift
123	    (Proj.awayι (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)
124	      ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] :
125	        Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) 1)
126	      (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_fDeg kbar 1)
127	      (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_hm 1))
128	    (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar).left ?_, ?_⟩
129	  · -- Range containment: image of `onePt.left` is `[1:1]`, which lies in `D₊(X 1)`.
130	    -- Reduces to `(evalIntoGlobal kbar (fun _ => 1)) (X 1) = 1` (a unit), so the
131	    -- preimage of `D₊(X 1)` under `onePt.left` is the entire `Spec k̄`.
132	    -- Step 1: rewrite `Set.range awayι = ↑(awayι.opensRange) = ↑(Proj.basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1))`.
133	    rw [← Scheme.Hom.coe_opensRange (Proj.awayι _ _ _ _), Proj.opensRange_awayι]
134	    -- Step 2: pointwise.
135	    rintro _ ⟨x, rfl⟩
136	    -- Goal: `onePt.left x ∈ ↑(Proj.basicOpen 𝒜 (X 1))`.
137	    -- Step 3: convert to preimage form, then apply
138	    -- `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_preimage_basicOpen`. The `onePt.left` morphism is
139	    -- definitionally `Proj.fromOfGlobalSections (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) φ hφ`
140	    -- where `φ = evalIntoGlobal kbar (fun _ => 1)` and `hφ = irrelevant_map_eq_top ...`
141	    -- (both private — but the kernel sees through `Over.homMk` + `pointOfVec`).
142	    rw [SetLike.mem_coe, ← Scheme.Hom.mem_preimage]
143	    change x ∈ Proj.fromOfGlobalSections (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar) _ _ ⁻¹ᵁ
144	        Proj.basicOpen (projectiveLineBarGrading kbar)
145	          ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] : Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) 1)
146	    rw [Proj.fromOfGlobalSections_preimage_basicOpen _ _ _
147	        (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_hm 1)
148	        (projectiveLineBarAffineCover_fDeg kbar 1)]
149	    -- Goal: `x ∈ (Spec k̄).basicOpen (evalIntoGlobal v (X 1))`. Since `v 1 = 1` and
150	    -- `evalIntoGlobal v` definitionally unfolds to `(ΓSpecIso).inv.hom ∘ eval v`, the
151	    -- argument is `(ΓSpecIso).inv.hom 1 = 1` — a unit; the basic open of a unit is `⊤`.
152	    refine (Scheme.basicOpen_of_isUnit _ ?_).symm ▸ TopologicalSpace.Opens.mem_top x
153	    -- Show the argument is a unit. Unfolding `evalIntoGlobal` (private but defeq):
154	    --   `evalIntoGlobal kbar (fun _ => 1) (X 1)
155	    --     = (ΓSpecIso _).inv.hom (eval (fun _ => 1) (X 1))
156	    --     = (ΓSpecIso _).inv.hom 1
157	    --     = 1`,
158	    -- so we can rewrite to `1` then close by `isUnit_one`.
159	    change IsUnit ((Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of kbar)).inv.hom
160	      ((MvPolynomial.eval (fun _ : Fin 2 => (1 : kbar)))
161	        ((![MvPolynomial.X 0, MvPolynomial.X 1] : Fin 2 → MvPolynomial (Fin 2) kbar) 1)))
162	    simp
163	  · exact IsOpenImmersion.lift_fac _ _ _
164	
165	/-- **Sub-task f reusable compatibility helper (iter-183 Lane E):** the compatibility
166	hypothesis for the inner `pullback.lift` of the section `s`, namely
167	`((Gm).hom ≫ onePt.left) ≫ PLB.hom = 𝟙 Gm.left ≫ Gm.hom`. Reduces to `Over.w onePt`

511	/-- **Helper (pointed case): a morphism `f : ℙ¹ → A` killing `0 ∈ ℙ¹` is the constant `1`.**
512	Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, if `f : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A` satisfies
513	`ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ f = η[A]`, then `f = (1 : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A)`
514	(equivalently `f = toUnit ProjectiveLineBar ≫ η[A]`).

516	This is the pointed core of the `𝔾ₘ`-scaling shortcut: form `h := gmScalingP1 ≫ f`, feed it
517	to Cor 1.5 (`hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity`) with `V = ProjectiveLineBar`, `W = Gm`, base
518	points `zeroPt`, `onePt`. The `W`-axis collapses by `gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero`, leaving
519	`h = fst ≫ fV` (the relation `f(λx) = fV(x)`). Specialising at `x = 1` (via the canonical
520	inclusion `Gm ↪ ℙ¹` given by `λ ↦ σ×(1, λ) = λ`) and using density of `Gm ⊆ ℙ¹` plus
521	separatedness of `A` (via `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`, the same Mathlib bridge
522	`rigidity_core` uses inline), we conclude `f = toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ (onePt ≫ fV)`. The basepoint

533	private theorem morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const_aux
534	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
535	    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
536	    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
537	    (f : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A)
538	    (hf0 : ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ f = η[A]) :
539	    f = (1 : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A) := by
540	  -- W-axis collapse via `gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero`, precomposed with `onePt`:
541	  -- `lift zeroPt onePt ≫ gmScalingP1 = zeroPt`.
542	  have hcollapse :
543	      lift (ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar) (Gm.onePt kbar) ≫ gmScalingP1 kbar
544	        = ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar := by
545	    have hbase := gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero kbar
546	    -- Rewrite `lift zeroPt onePt = onePt ≫ lift (toUnit Gm ≫ zeroPt) (𝟙 Gm)`.
547	    have hreshape :
548	        lift (ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar) (Gm.onePt kbar)
549	          = Gm.onePt kbar ≫
550	            lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar) (𝟙 (Gm kbar)) := by
551	      rw [comp_lift, Category.comp_id]
552	      congr 1
553	      rw [← Category.assoc,
554	        toUnit_unique (Gm.onePt kbar ≫ toUnit (Gm kbar)) (𝟙 _), Category.id_comp]
555	    rw [hreshape, Category.assoc, hbase, ← Category.assoc,
556	      toUnit_unique (Gm.onePt kbar ≫ toUnit (Gm kbar)) (𝟙 _), Category.id_comp]
557	  -- The Cor 1.5 basepoint hypothesis `lift v₀ w₀ ≫ (σ ≫ f) = η[A]`.
558	  have hcorhyp :
559	      lift (ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar) (Gm.onePt kbar)
560	        ≫ (gmScalingP1 kbar ≫ f) = η[A] := by
561	    rw [← Category.assoc, hcollapse]; exact hf0
562	  -- Apply Cor 1.5.
563	  have key := hom_additive_decomp_of_rigidity
564	    (V := ProjectiveLineBar kbar) (W := Gm kbar)
565	    (ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar) (Gm.onePt kbar)
566	    (gmScalingP1 kbar ≫ f) hcorhyp
567	  -- W-axis restriction: `lift (toUnit Gm ≫ zeroPt) (𝟙 Gm) ≫ σ ≫ f = (1 : Gm ⟶ A)`.
568	  have hfW :
569	      lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar) (𝟙 (Gm kbar))
570	        ≫ (gmScalingP1 kbar ≫ f) = (1 : Gm kbar ⟶ A) := by
571	    rw [← Category.assoc, gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero, Category.assoc, hf0]
572	    exact Hom.one_def.symm
573	  -- `snd ≫ fW = snd ≫ 1 = 1` (using `toUnit` uniqueness).
574	  have hSndFW :
575	      snd (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) (Gm kbar)
576	        ≫ (lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar) (𝟙 (Gm kbar))
577	          ≫ (gmScalingP1 kbar ≫ f))
578	        = (1 : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⊗ Gm kbar ⟶ A) := by
579	    rw [hfW, Hom.one_def, ← Category.assoc]
580	    congr 1
581	    exact toUnit_unique _ _
582	  -- `key` now reads `σ ≫ f = fst ≫ fV` (after collapsing the W-axis to 1).
583	  rw [hSndFW, _root_.mul_one] at key
584	  -- Name the V-axis restriction `fV`.
585	  set fV : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A :=
586	    lift (𝟙 (ProjectiveLineBar kbar)) (toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫ Gm.onePt kbar)
587	      ≫ (gmScalingP1 kbar ≫ f) with hfVdef
588	  -- Precompose `key` with `gmInP1 := lift (toUnit Gm ≫ onePt) (𝟙 Gm) : Gm → ℙ¹ ⊗ Gm`
589	  -- ("λ ↦ (1, λ)"). The resulting morphism `ι := gmInP1 ≫ σ : Gm → ℙ¹` is the canonical
590	  -- inclusion `Gm ↪ ℙ¹` ("λ ↦ σ×(1, λ) = λ"); on its image, `f` is constant at `onePt ≫ fV`.
591	  set iotaGm : Gm kbar ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar kbar :=
592	    lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar) (𝟙 (Gm kbar))
593	      ≫ gmScalingP1 kbar with hιdef
594	  have hιf :
595	      iotaGm ≫ f = toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar ≫ fV) := by
596	    calc iotaGm ≫ f
597	        = lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar) (𝟙 (Gm kbar))
598	            ≫ (gmScalingP1 kbar ≫ f) := by rw [hιdef, Category.assoc]
599	      _ = lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar) (𝟙 (Gm kbar))
600	            ≫ (fst (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) (Gm kbar) ≫ fV) := by rw [key]
601	      _ = (lift (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar) (𝟙 (Gm kbar))
602	            ≫ fst (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) (Gm kbar)) ≫ fV := by
603	          rw [Category.assoc]
604	      _ = (toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar) ≫ fV := by rw [lift_fst]
605	      _ = toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar ≫ fV) := by
606	          rw [Category.assoc]
607	  -- Separatedness of the target `A` over `Spec k̄` in `OverClass.fromOver` form.
608	  haveI hAsep : IsSeparated A.hom := IsProper.toIsSeparated
609	  haveI : IsSeparated (A.left ↘ Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)) := hAsep
610	  -- `IsReduced (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left` and the product geom-irred / LOFT / IsReduced
611	  -- instances are all auto-resolved via Lane A's exports in `Genus0BaseObjects`
612	  -- (`projectiveLineBar_isReduced`, `projGm_geomIrred`, `projGm_locallyOfFiniteType`,
613	  -- `projGm_isReduced`). Dominance of `ι.left` is the only file-local bridge, cited
614	  -- explicitly via `iotaGm_isDominant` (the `set`-binding `hιdef` exposes `iotaGm.left`
615	  -- definitionally as `(lift _ _ ≫ gmScalingP1 kbar).left`).
616	  haveI hιDom : IsDominant iotaGm.left := iotaGm_isDominant
617	  -- Globalise: `ι ≫ f = ι ≫ (toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ (onePt ≫ fV))` gives, by dominance, `f = toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ c`.
618	  have hf_eq :
619	      f = toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar ≫ fV) := by
620	    -- Promote the Over morphism equality from the underlying scheme equality.
621	    have hgoal :
622	        iotaGm ≫ f
623	          = iotaGm ≫ (toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫
624	              (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar ≫ fV)) := by
625	      have hreshape :
626	          iotaGm ≫ (toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫
627	              (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar ≫ fV))
628	            = toUnit (Gm kbar) ≫ (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar ≫ fV) := by
629	        rw [← Category.assoc,
630	          toUnit_unique (iotaGm ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar)) (toUnit (Gm kbar))]
631	      rw [hreshape, hιf]
632	    refine Over.OverMorphism.ext ?_
633	    refine ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated' (S := Spec (.of kbar))
634	      (X := (ProjectiveLineBar kbar).left) (Y := A.left)
635	      (f := f.left)
636	      (g := (toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫
637	        (ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar ≫ fV)).left) iotaGm.left ?_
638	    rw [← Over.comp_left, ← Over.comp_left]
639	    exact congrArg Over.Hom.left hgoal
640	  -- Pin `onePt ≫ fV = η[A]` via the basepoint hypothesis `hf0`.
641	  have hc : ProjectiveLineBar.onePt kbar ≫ fV = η[A] := by
642	    have hcomp := hf0
643	    rw [hf_eq] at hcomp
644	    rw [← Category.assoc,
645	      toUnit_unique (ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar))
646	        (𝟙 _),
647	      Category.id_comp] at hcomp
648	    exact hcomp
649	  rw [hf_eq, hc, ← Hom.one_def]

651	/-- **A morphism `ℙ¹ → A` is constant.** Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, every
652	morphism `f : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A` from the projective line into an abelian variety
653	`A` (a smooth proper geometrically irreducible group scheme) is constant: it factors through
654	a single `k̄`-point `a₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ A`, i.e. `f = toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ a₀`.
655	
656	The single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition 3.10. **Route resolved iter-164: the
657	𝔾ₘ-scaling shortcut** — NO theorem of the cube, NO Milne Thm 3.2, NO `Hom(𝔾ₐ, A) = 0`,
658	char-general. The proof body proceeds by translating in the group `A` to reduce to the
659	basepoint case `zeroPt ≫ f = η[A]` (handled by the helper
660	`morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const_aux`), then un-translating.

662	See blueprint `prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant`
663	(Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, Prop. 3.10).

666	(three product-instance Mathlib bridges + dominance of the canonical `Gm → ℙ¹` map). Lifts

668	theorem morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const
669	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
670	    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
671	    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
672	    (f : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A) :
673	    ∃ a₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ A,
674	      f = toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫ a₀ := by
675	  -- Witness: `a₀ := zeroPt ≫ f` (the value of `f` at the scaling fixed point `0 ∈ ℙ¹`).
676	  refine ⟨ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ f, ?_⟩
677	  -- Translate: form `f' := f / (toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ a₀)` and apply the helper to `f'`.
678	  have hf' :
679	      (f / (toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫ ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ f))
680	        = (1 : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A) := by
681	    apply morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const_aux
682	    -- Show `zeroPt ≫ (f / (toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ a₀)) = η[A]`.
683	    rw [GrpObj.comp_div]
684	    have hv :
685	        ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫
686	            toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫
687	              (ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ f)
688	          = ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ f := by
689	      rw [← Category.assoc,
690	        toUnit_unique (ProjectiveLineBar.zeroPt kbar ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar))
691	          (𝟙 _),
692	        Category.id_comp]
693	    rw [hv, div_self', Hom.one_def, toUnit_unique (toUnit _) (𝟙 _), Category.id_comp]
694	  -- Untranslate: `f / (toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ a₀) = 1` ⟺ `f = toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ a₀`.
695	  exact div_eq_one.mp hf'

718	/-- **Headline: rigidity for a pointed genus-`0` curve.** Over an algebraically closed field
719	`k̄` (arbitrary characteristic — no `[CharZero kbar]`), let `C` be a smooth proper geometrically
720	irreducible curve with `genus C = 0` and `A` an abelian variety (smooth proper geom-irred group
721	scheme). Then every morphism `f : C ⟶ A` killing a `k̄`-point `p` (`p ≫ f = η[A]`) equals the
722	constant morphism at the identity, `f = toUnit C ≫ η[A]`.
723	
724	This is the project's committed characteristic-free statement that `genusZeroWitness` consumes
725	(via the `k̄ → k` descent step hosted in `AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian`). Its signature mirrors
726	`AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar` of `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar` **verbatim except**
727	the `[CharZero kbar]` instance is dropped. Combine `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` (`C ≅ ℙ¹`) with
728	`morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` (`ℙ¹ → A` constant) and pin the constant value to `η[A]` via
729	the pointed hypothesis. No `df = 0`, no Serre duality, no Picard representability.

735	theorem rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme
736	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
737	    {C : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
738	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom]
739	    [IsProper C.hom]
740	    [GeometricallyIrreducible C.hom]
741	    (_hgenus : genus C = 0)
742	    {A : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
743	    [GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]
744	    (f : C ⟶ A)
745	    (p : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ C)
746	    (_hf : p ≫ f = η[A]) :
747	    f = (toUnit C ≫ η[A]) := by
748	  -- Transport `f` along `C ≅ ProjectiveLineBar kbar` to a morphism `g : ℙ¹ ⟶ A`.
749	  obtain ⟨φ⟩ := genusZero_curve_iso_P1 _hgenus
750	  set g : ProjectiveLineBar kbar ⟶ A := φ.inv ≫ f with hgdef
751	  -- `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` gives `g = toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ a₀` for some `a₀`.
752	  obtain ⟨a₀, hga₀⟩ := morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const g
753	  -- Pin `a₀ = η[A]` via the pointed hypothesis on `f`.
754	  have hpoint : (p ≫ φ.hom) ≫ g = η[A] := by
755	    rw [hgdef, Category.assoc, ← Category.assoc φ.hom, φ.hom_inv_id, Category.id_comp]
756	    exact _hf
757	  have hcst : a₀ = η[A] := by
758	    rw [hga₀, ← Category.assoc,
759	      toUnit_unique ((p ≫ φ.hom) ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar)) (𝟙 _),
760	      Category.id_comp] at hpoint
761	    exact hpoint
762	  rw [hcst] at hga₀
763	  -- Back-transport: `f = φ.hom ≫ g = φ.hom ≫ toUnit ℙ¹ ≫ η[A] = toUnit C ≫ η[A]`.
764	  calc f
765	      = φ.hom ≫ φ.inv ≫ f := by
766	        rw [← Category.assoc, φ.hom_inv_id, Category.id_comp]
767	    _ = φ.hom ≫ g := by rw [hgdef]
768	    _ = φ.hom ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar) ≫ η[A] := by rw [hga₀]
769	    _ = toUnit C ≫ η[A] := by
770	        rw [← Category.assoc,
771	          toUnit_unique (φ.hom ≫ toUnit (ProjectiveLineBar kbar)) (toUnit C)]

774	end AlgebraicGeometry

923	2. For each `c < d`, the quotient `R / (x_1, …, x_c)` is again a regular local

950	/-- **Substrate gap consolidated (Stacks 00NQ + 00NU; typed `sorry`).**
951	For a regular local ring `(R, 𝔪)` of Krull dimension `k + 1`, there exists
952	`x ∈ 𝔪` that is `R`-regular (a non-zero-divisor on `R`) such that the quotient
953	`R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` is again a regular local ring of Krull dimension `k`
954	(equivalently: its maximal ideal has `spanFinrank = k`).

956	The internals combine two Stacks gap-fills, both absent from Mathlib at the

984	/-- **Lane G inductive step (iter-185 structural scaffold).** Packages the
985	inductive step of Stacks 00OD: given a regular local ring `R` of dimension
986	`k + 1`, plus the inductive hypothesis at dimension `k` (universally
987	quantified in the ring), produce a regular sequence of length `k + 1` in
988	the maximal ideal of `R`.
989	
990	The body is **axiom-clean** modulo the typed-`sorry` substrate helper
991	`exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ`. Assembly path:
992	
993	1. Helper extracts `x ∈ 𝔪` with `IsSMulRegular R x` AND `IsRegularLocalRing
994	   (R⧸(x))` of `spanFinrank = k`.
995	2. IH applied on `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` produces a regular sequence
996	   `rs'_q : List (R ⧸ (x))` of length `k` in the maximal ideal there.
997	3. Lift `rs'_q` to `rs : List R` via `Function.surjInv` of
998	   `Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective`; the section property gives
999	   `rs.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk _) = rs'_q`.
1000	4. Members of `rs` lie in `𝔪` because the maximal ideal of `R⧸(x)` is the
1001	   image of `𝔪` (`IsLocalRing.le_maximalIdeal` applied to the comap chain).
1002	5. Cons via `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.cons'` to form the length-`(k+1)`
1003	   sequence `x :: rs`.
1004	
1005	This factoring isolates the Mathlib gap to one narrow typed `sorry` instead of
1006	spreading it across the lemma body; future iterations can attack Stacks 00NQ
1007	+ 00NU directly. -/
1008	private lemma regularLocal_inductive_step {R : Type u} [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1009	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] {k : ℕ}
1010	    (hdim : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = k + 1)
1011	    (IH : ∀ (R' : Type u) [CommRing R'] [IsLocalRing R'] [IsNoetherianRing R']
1012	            [IsRegularLocalRing R'],
1013	            (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R').spanFinrank = k →
1014	            ∃ rs : List R', rs.length = k ∧
1015	              (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R') ∧
1016	              RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R' rs) :
1017	    ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = k + 1 ∧
1018	      (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ∧
1019	      RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs := by
1020	  -- Step 1: extract `x ∈ 𝔪` regular on `R` with `R ⧸ (x)` regular local of
1021	  -- `spanFinrank = k`.  This is the consolidated Stacks 00NQ + 00NU substrate.
1022	  obtain ⟨x, hxMem, hxReg, hRLR, hdim_quot⟩ :=
1023	    exists_isSMulRegular_quotient_isRegularLocal_succ hdim
1024	  -- Step 2: apply IH on `R ⧸ (x)` — this gives a regular sequence of length
1025	  -- `k` on the quotient, valued in its maximal ideal.
1026	  obtain ⟨rs'_q, hlen_q, hmem_q, hreg_q⟩ := IH (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}) hdim_quot
1027	  -- Step 3: lift `rs'_q : List (R ⧸ (x))` to `rs' : List R` via the right
1028	  -- inverse of the (surjective) quotient ring hom.
1029	  let mkq : R →+* R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} := Ideal.Quotient.mk _
1030	  let g : R ⧸ Ideal.span {x} → R := Function.surjInv Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
1031	  have hg : ∀ y, mkq (g y) = y := Function.surjInv_eq _
1032	  let rs' : List R := rs'_q.map g
1033	  have hlen_rs' : rs'.length = k := by simp [rs', hlen_q]
1034	  have hmkmap : rs'.map mkq = rs'_q := by
1035	    change (rs'_q.map g).map mkq = rs'_q
1036	    rw [List.map_map]
1037	    conv_rhs => rw [← List.map_id rs'_q]
1038	    exact List.map_congr_left fun y _ => hg y
1039	  -- Step 4: each element of `rs'` lies in `𝔪 R` via the comap of `𝔪 (R⧸(x))`.
1040	  -- The maximal ideal of `R ⧸ (x)` comaps back to `𝔪 R` (it's the *unique*
1041	  -- maximal ideal of `R` containing `Ideal.span {x} ⊆ 𝔪`).
1042	  have hmem_rs' : ∀ r ∈ rs', r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by
1043	    intro r hr
1044	    simp only [rs', List.mem_map] at hr
1045	    obtain ⟨y, hy_mem, rfl⟩ := hr
1046	    -- The comap of `𝔪 (R⧸(x))` under the surjective `mkq` is a maximal ideal
1047	    -- of `R`; since `R` is local, it equals `𝔪 R`.  Hence `g y ∈ comap mkq 𝔪'`
1048	    -- iff `g y ∈ 𝔪 R`.
1049	    have hmax_comap : (Ideal.comap mkq
1050	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}))).IsMaximal :=
1051	      Ideal.comap_isMaximal_of_surjective _ Ideal.Quotient.mk_surjective
1052	    have heq : Ideal.comap mkq
1053	        (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}))
1054	        = IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R :=
1055	      (IsLocalRing.isMaximal_iff R).mp hmax_comap
1056	    rw [← heq, Ideal.mem_comap]
1057	    exact (hg y).symm ▸ hmem_q y hy_mem
1058	  -- Step 5: cons `x` onto `rs'` to form the length-`(k+1)` regular sequence.
1059	  refine ⟨x :: rs', ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
1060	  · simp [hlen_rs']
1061	  · intro r hr
1062	    rcases List.mem_cons.mp hr with rfl | hr_in
1063	    · exact hxMem
1064	    · exact hmem_rs' r hr_in
1065	  · -- `IsRegular R (x :: rs')` via `IsRegular.cons'`.
1066	    -- Need `IsSMulRegular R x` (have `hxReg`) AND
1067	    -- `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x R) (rs'.map (Ideal.Quotient.mk (Ideal.span {x})))`.
1068	    refine RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.cons' hxReg ?_
1069	    -- After `cons'`: goal is `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x R) (rs'.map mkq)`
1070	    -- = `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x R) rs'_q` (by `hmkmap`), implicit ring
1071	    -- `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` (inferred from list type).
1072	    rw [hmkmap]
1073	    -- Goal: `IsRegular (QuotSMulTop x R) rs'_q` (implicit ring `R ⧸ (x)`).
1074	    -- IH provides: `IsRegular (R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}) rs'_q` (same implicit
1075	    -- ring, but M differs: `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` vs `QuotSMulTop x R = R ⧸ (x • ⊤)`).
1076	    --
1077	    -- **iter-185 typed `sorry` — TECHNICAL BRIDGE (NOT a Mathlib substrate gap).**
1078	    -- The two M's are *equal as sets* — both are the quotient of `R` by the
1079	    -- principal ideal `(x)`, written two different ways:
1080	    --   * `R ⧸ Ideal.span {x}` — quotient by `Ideal.span {x}` as a Submodule R R;
1081	    --   * `QuotSMulTop x R = R ⧸ (x • ⊤)` — quotient by `x • ⊤` as a Submodule R R.
1082	    -- Mathlib's `Submodule.ideal_span_singleton_smul` gives
1083	    -- `(x • ⊤ : Submodule R R) = Ideal.span {x} • ⊤ = Ideal.span {x}` (the
1084	    -- second equality because `I • ⊤_R = I` for an ideal `I` of `R`).  The
1085	    -- bridge between the two M's is an `R⧸(x)`-linear equivalence; future iter
1086	    -- can construct this in ~10-20 LOC via:
1087	    --   (a) `Submodule.quotEquivOfEq` from the submodule equality (gives R-linear);
1088	    --   (b) upgrade to `R⧸(x)`-linear via `QuotSMulTop.mem_annihilator`
1089	    --       (since `x ∈ Module.annihilator R (QuotSMulTop x R)`, the R-action
1090	    --       factors through `R⧸(x)`, and any R-linear equiv between two such
1091	    --       modules is automatically `R⧸(x)`-linear);
1092	    --   (c) close via `LinearEquiv.isRegular_congr` on this `R⧸(x)`-linear equiv.
1093	    -- This bridge has no substrate dependencies — it's pure bookkeeping.
1094	    sorry
1095	
1096	lemma exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal
1097	    (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R] [IsNoetherianRing R]
1098	    [IsRegularLocalRing R] :
1099	    ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank
1100	        ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
1101	        ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs := by
1102	  -- Strong induction on `n = spanFinrank R`, generalising `R` so the inductive
1103	  -- hypothesis can be applied to the quotient `R/(x)` at smaller dimension.
1104	  suffices haux : ∀ (n : ℕ) (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1105	      [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R],
1106	      (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank = n →
1107	      ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧
1108	        (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) ∧
1109	        RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs by
1110	    exact haux _ R rfl
1111	  intro n
1112	  induction n with
1113	  | zero =>
1114	    -- Base case `dim 0`: spanFinrank = 0, hence `maximalIdeal R = ⊥` (so `R` is
1115	    -- a field). The empty list is trivially `R`-regular on the nonzero ring `R`.
1116	    intros R _ _ _ _ _hdim
1117	    refine ⟨[], rfl, by simp, ?_⟩
1118	    exact RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular.nil R R
1119	  | succ k ih =>
1120	    -- Inductive case `dim (k + 1)`: delegate to `regularLocal_inductive_step`,
1121	    -- supplying the inductive hypothesis at dimension `k`. The helper handles
1122	    -- the substantive NZD-extraction + quotient-regularity + cons assembly.
1123	    intros R _ _ _ _ hdim
1124	    exact regularLocal_inductive_step (k := k) hdim (fun R' _ _ _ _ h => ih R' h)
1125	
1126	/-- **Regular local rings are Cohen–Macaulay.** Every regular Noetherian
1127	local ring is Cohen–Macaulay: a minimal generating set of `𝔪` is an
1128	`R`-regular sequence of length `dim R`, so `depth(R) ≥ dim R`; combined
1129	with the standard upper bound `depth(R) ≤ dim R` (Stacks 00LK) this gives
1130	`depth(R) = dim R`.
1131	
1132	This is the consumer-facing input for **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
1133	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective
1134	surface): the local ring `O_{S,x}` of a regular projective surface at a
1135	closed point is regular of Krull dimension `2`, hence Cohen–Macaulay,
1136	hence has depth `2`, which is exactly the input the local-cohomology
1137	vanishing `H^i_x(O_S) = 0` for `i < 2` needs (Stacks 0AVF; see Hartshorne
1138	III.7).
1139	
1140	iter-181 Lane G **STRUCTURAL**: body is now decomposed into two
1141	typed helper lemmas — `length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular` (the upper
1142	bound, closed kernel-clean from
1143	`ringKrullDim_add_length_eq_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular`) and
1144	`exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal` (the lower bound, typed `sorry` on the
1145	Mathlib gap `IsRegularLocalRing ⟹ IsDomain` + regular-quotient induction).
1146	The combined assembly into `depth = ringKrullDim` is closed inline below,
1147	so the only residual `sorry` in this `instance` body is the named helper. -/
1148	instance of_regular (R : Type u) [CommRing R] [IsLocalRing R]
1149	    [IsNoetherianRing R] [IsRegularLocalRing R] : CohenMacaulay R where
1150	  depth_eq_krullDim := by
1151	    -- Step 1: simplify `Module.depth` via the `else` branch
1152	    --   (since `𝔪 • ⊤ = 𝔪 ≠ ⊤` for a local ring's maximal ideal).
1153	    have h𝔪 : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R R)
1154	        ≠ (⊤ : Submodule R R) := by
1155	      have heq : (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) • (⊤ : Submodule R R)
1156	          = IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R := by simp
1157	      rw [heq]
1158	      exact (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal.isMaximal R).ne_top
1159	    rw [Module.depth, if_neg h𝔪]
1160	    -- Step 2: convert RHS to the spanFinrank using
1161	    -- `IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal`.
1162	    rw [← IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal]
1163	    -- Goal: ((sSup {n | …} : ℕ∞) : WithBot ℕ∞)
1164	    --         = ((spanFinrank 𝔪 : ℕ) : WithBot ℕ∞)
1165	    -- Step 3: it suffices to show the sSup equals spanFinrank as ℕ∞,
1166	    -- via antisymmetry: upper bound from Helper 1, lower bound from Helper 2.
1167	    have h1 : (sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
1168	        (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R)
1169	          ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular R rs }
1170	        : ℕ∞) = ((IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R).spanFinrank : ℕ∞) := by
1171	      apply le_antisymm
1172	      · -- Upper bound: every element of the sSup-set is at most spanFinrank.
1173	        apply sSup_le
1174	        rintro n ⟨rs, rfl, _, hreg⟩
1175	        have hub := length_le_ringKrullDim_of_isRegular hreg
1176	        rw [← IsRegularLocalRing.spanFinrank_maximalIdeal] at hub
1177	        exact_mod_cast hub

1179	        obtain ⟨rs, hlen, hmem, hreg⟩ := exists_isRegular_of_regularLocal R

9	# Auslander–Buchsbaum formula (A.4.b)
10	
11	This file is the **A.4.b** file-skeleton sub-build chapter for the project's
12	positive-genus arm of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It packages the
13	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula and the corollary "regular local ring
14	⟹ Cohen–Macaulay" that the sibling sub-build **A.4.a** (codim-1 extension of a
15	rational map across a codim-2 closed point on a regular projective surface)
16	consumes.
17	
18	Per STRATEGY.md L30 this row is gated downstream on A.4.a but is independently
19	startable on the Mathlib side: the algebra here is decoupled from the Albanese
20	geometry and lives entirely under `RingTheory.*`. Mathlib at the project's
21	pinned commit (`b80f227`) exposes `IsRegularLocalRing` and the categorical
22	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` on an abelian category (specialised to
23	`ModuleCat R`), as well as the regular-sequence definition
24	`RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`. The depth function `Module.depth`, the
25	Auslander–Buchsbaum formula, and the Cohen–Macaulay predicate are *not* in
26	Mathlib at the pinned commit — they are the new content scaffolded here.
27

112	/-! ## §1. Depth of a finite module
113	
114	The `I`-depth of a finite `R`-module `M` is the supremum in
115	`{0, 1, 2, …, ∞}` of the lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in `I`
116	(provided `IM ≠ M`; if `IM = M` we set `depth_I(M) = ∞`). Mathlib `b80f227`
117	exposes the regular-sequence predicate `RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular`
118	(`Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.RegularSequence`) but not the resulting
119	numeric depth function — that is the gap this declaration fills.
120	
121	Blueprint reference: `def:depth` (Stacks tag 00LF). -/
122	
123	/-- The **`I`-depth** of a finite `R`-module `M`: the supremum (in `ℕ∞`) of
124	lengths of `M`-regular sequences contained in the ideal `I`.
125	
126	When `IM = M` (the "trivial-quotient" case, e.g. `M = 0` or `I = R`) the
127	supremum is taken to be `⊤` by convention. When `(R, 𝔪)` is local one usually
128	calls `depth (IsLocalRing.maximalIdeal R) M` simply *the depth* of `M`.
129	
130	iter-176+: the body is the supremum
131	```
132	sSup { (n : ℕ∞) | ∃ rs : List R, rs.length = n ∧ (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ I) ∧
133	                  RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular M rs }
134	```
135	folded with the `IM = M` clause. The signature is non-tautological: it
136	asserts a function `(Ideal R) → (M : Type v) → ℕ∞` matching the Stacks
137	00LF definition.
138	
139	**iter-179 Mathlib-gap check (Lane F Target 2 STRETCH)**: a pinned-commit
140	audit of `Mathlib.RingTheory.Regular.Depth` (the only file under
141	`Mathlib/RingTheory/*` containing the word "depth") confirms that Mathlib
142	`b80f227` ships only depth-zero lemmas (`IsSMulRegular.subsingleton_linearMap_iff`)
143	and *not* the numeric depth function itself. The one-liner re-export route
144	is therefore not available; the body stays a typed `sorry` until an
145	iter-180+ body lane fills the supremum-with-`IM=M` clause directly. -/
146	noncomputable def depth {R : Type u} [CommRing R] (_I : Ideal R)
147	    (_M : Type v) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : ℕ∞ :=
148	  open Classical in
149	  if _I • (⊤ : Submodule R _M) = ⊤ then (⊤ : ℕ∞)
150	  else sSup { n : ℕ∞ | ∃ rs : List R, (rs.length : ℕ∞) = n ∧
151	    (∀ r ∈ rs, r ∈ _I) ∧ RingTheory.Sequence.IsRegular _M rs }
152	
153	end Module
154	
155	end RingTheory
156	
157	/-! ## §2. Projective dimension
158	
159	Mathlib `b80f227` exposes the categorical
160	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension : C → WithBot ℕ∞` on an abelian category
161	with enough projectives (file `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Abelian.Projective.Dimension`).
162	For `R`-modules this specialises to `ModuleCat.of R M`. The blueprint pins the
163	`Module.projectiveDimension` name as the re-export that downstream consumers
164	can use directly on an `R`-module without first packaging it in `ModuleCat`.
165	
166	Blueprint reference: `def:projective_dimension`. -/
167	
168	namespace Module
169	
170	/-- The **projective dimension** of an `R`-module `M`, defined as the
171	categorical projective dimension of `ModuleCat.of R M`.
172	
173	This is a re-export of `CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension` specialised to
174	the abelian category `ModuleCat R`. The categorical definition is the
175	infimum (in `WithBot ℕ∞`) of `n : ℕ` such that all `Ext^i(M, -)` vanish for
176	`i > n`, equivalently the smallest length of a projective resolution of `M`.
177	
178	Mathlib has the categorical `projectiveDimension` and the module-specific
179	`ModuleCat.projectiveDimension_eq_of_linearEquiv`; the blueprint pins the
180	wrapper name so downstream consumers can write
181	`Module.projectiveDimension R M` rather than threading `ModuleCat.of`.
182	
183	The body is the one-line re-export
184	`CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)`; iter-178 closed
185	this declaration kernel-clean. -/
186	noncomputable def projectiveDimension (R : Type u) [Ring R]
187	    (_M : Type u) [AddCommGroup _M] [Module R _M] : WithBot ℕ∞ :=
188	  CategoryTheory.projectiveDimension (ModuleCat.of R _M)
189	
190	end Module
191	
192	namespace RingTheory
193	
194	namespace Module
195	
196	/-! ## §3. Depth via Ext characterisation
197	
198	For a Noetherian local ring `(R, 𝔪)` with residue field `κ = R/𝔪` and a
199	nonzero finite `R`-module `M`, the depth of `M` equals the smallest index `i`
200	at which `Ext^i_R(κ, M)` is nonzero (Stacks tag 00LP).

11	# Genus-`0` base objects (Stratum 4): the `𝔾_m`-scaling action `σ_×` and product-stability instances
12	
13	This file is **Stratum 4** of the four-stratum split of the legacy
14	`AlgebraicJacobian.Genus0BaseObjects` (iter-175 refactor `g0bo-split`). It ships:
15	
16	* the chart-bridge `awayι_comp_PLB_hom` (iter-173 `chart-bridge173` recipe step (a));
17	* the per-chart ring maps `gmScalingP1_chart{0,1}_ringMap` of the scaling action;
18	* the pullback cover `gmScalingP1_cover` of `(ℙ¹ ⊗ 𝔾_m).left`;
19	* the per-chart scheme morphism `gmScalingP1_chart`, the cocycle agreement
20	  `gmScalingP1_chart_agreement`, and the over-coherence
21	  `gmScalingP1_over_coherence`;
22	* the bare scaling morphism `gmScalingP1 : ProjectiveLineBar ⊗ Gm ⟶ ProjectiveLineBar`;
23	* the load-bearing fixed-point property `gmScalingP1_collapse_at_zero`;
24	* the product-stability instances on `ℙ¹ ⊗ 𝔾_m` exported for Lane B in
25	  `AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean`.
26	
27	Upstream strata: `BareScheme`, `ChartIso`, `Points`.
28	-/
